ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for pho bo tai; closest parent 'noodle soup food product' (FOODON_00002700) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP for beef noodle soup used as closest parent; no specific pho bo tai code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for pho bo tai or Vietnamese beef noodle soup.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant bowl of pho bo tai weighs approximately 500 g, comprising broth, rice noodles, thinly sliced rare beef, and garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: Pho (Vietnamese beef noodle soup) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite; cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Vietnamese dishes
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA entry exists for pho bo tai as a restaurant dish. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for beef noodle soup (NDB 22899 / FDC ID 172944) and Vietnamese pho composite data from FAO/INFOODS SEA tables. Per-100g values reflect a typical restaurant-prepared pho bo tai with broth, rice noodles, and rare beef slices at approximately 60% broth, 25% noodles, 15% beef by weight.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice noodles and beef broth composite estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pho bo tai as a composite dish. GI estimated at 45 based on the dominant glycemic component: rice noodles (GI approximately 53 per University of Sydney GI Database, Atkinson et al. 2008 Diabetes Care) diluted by the high-protein beef and large broth volume. The composite dish GI is estimated conservatively at 45 (low category). Glycemic load per 500 g serving calculated as: (45 × 30 g available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 13.5.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from beef broth and fish sauce
hypertension.guidance: A single 500 g bowl of pho bo tai provides approximately 1450 mg sodium, which represents over 60% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium broth or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice noodles offset by protein and broth volume
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pho bo tai has a low GI of approximately 45 and a moderate glycemic load of 13.5 per bowl; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes can include it in a meal plan but should monitor portion size of noodles and avoid adding hoisin sauce, which significantly raises the glycemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from beef
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (~1450 mg per bowl) and moderate saturated fat (~2.5 g per bowl) are relevant for cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption and choose lean beef cuts with reduced-sodium broth where available.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pho Bo Tai (Phở Bò Tái) is a Vietnamese rice noodle soup featuring thinly sliced raw or rare beef that cooks in the hot broth upon serving. The dish originates from northern Vietnam and is one of the most iconic Vietnamese dishes globally. The broth is prepared by simmering beef bones for many hours with charred ginger, onion, star anise, cinnamon, cloves, and cardamom, producing a deeply aromatic, clear, amber-coloured stock. Flat rice noodles (bánh phở) are placed in the bowl, topped with paper-thin slices of raw beef (tái means rare), and the boiling broth is ladled over to gently cook the meat. The dish is served with a garnish plate of bean sprouts, fresh Thai basil, sawtooth herb, lime wedges, and sliced chilli. Diners customise the flavour with hoisin sauce and sriracha. In Singapore, pho bo tai is widely available at Vietnamese restaurants and food courts. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-energy dish (~260 kcal per bowl) with a good protein content (~21 g per bowl) from the beef, moderate carbohydrate from rice noodles, and low fat. The primary nutritional concern is sodium, which is high due to the long-simmered bone broth and fish sauce seasoning. The dish is gluten-free and dairy-free, making it suitable for those with gluten intolerance. Its low-to-moderate glycemic index makes it a reasonable choice for individuals managing blood glucose, provided noodle portions are controlled.

confidence_reason: No exact match for pho bo tai as a restaurant dish exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central beef noodle soup entries and FAO/INFOODS SEA composite data. The per-100g macros are consistent with published Vietnamese pho composition data but represent an estimate for a restaurant-prepared dish. GI is an analogous estimate based on rice noodle GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central or FAO/INFOODS SEA source data for this dish. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the source database for this composite dish. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the source database. Trans fat is 0.0; confirmed as negligible/trace in lean beef and rice noodle preparations per USDA data. Halal status is marked false as the restaurant (Thien Long Vietnam Restaurant) has not been confirmed as halal-certified; consumers should verify directly. The dish is served at Thien Long Vietnam Restaurant, Circular Road, Singapore, as sourced from GrabFood Singapore (price SGD 11.90). Pho bo tai is classified under Vietnamese cuisine despite being served in Singapore; cuisine field reflects dish origin. The aliases_by_language entries for Tamil include 'வெட்டப்பட்ட மாட்டிறைச்சி நூடுல் சூப் (ஃபோ போ டாய்)' and 'கோ போ டாய்'; both appear verbatim in the flat aliases list. Hindi entries 'कटा हुआ बीफ नूडल सूप (फो बो ताई)' and 'फो बो ताई' both appear verbatim in the flat aliases list.
